name: Jomiro Eming
quote: "You do too many different things. No-one will know what you do. You need to find a niche."
bio:
Jomiro curates The Wrong List. He's a graphic designer, creative strategist, writer, and workshop facilitator — and yes, he's heard the niche speech.

He grew up between Germany and South Africa, which probably explains a lot. Two cultures, two ways of seeing, and a persistent inability to just pick one thing and stick to it. He studied journalism in Cape Town, moved into design, and has spent his career treating them as the same discipline: figure out what's true, then make it land.

Along the way he built Link Out, a community for freelancers in Hamburg and Berlin, because he kept meeting people who were doing interesting work in isolation and thought that seemed like a waste. He's written a book, How Birds Learn to Fly, about failure, freelancing, and what antifragility actually looks like when you're building a creative career from scratch.

He works with founders, organisations, and creative teams who need someone to think alongside them — across strategy, story, and how things look. People who have usually been told, at some point, that they're doing too much.

name: Lena Fischer
quote: "You have a perfectly good engineering career. Why would you throw that away?"
bio:
Lena spent seven years calculating load-bearing tolerances for bridges before she admitted she was more interested in how people moved across them than whether they'd hold.

The pivot made no sense on paper. She had a stable job, a mortgage, and a mother who still introduces her as 'my daughter, the engineer'. She retrained anyway.

She now designs complex digital systems — mostly for public infrastructure and civic tech — and argues that structural thinking is exactly what UX has been missing. Constraints aren't the opposite of creativity. They're the point.

She's based in Zurich, works mostly in German and English, and is occasionally asked to explain what interaction design is to her mother.

name: Maya Chen
quote: "You're too emotional to lead a team."
bio:
Maya built three companies before she turned 35. Each one was written off by at least one investor as 'too niche', 'too weird', or 'too early'. All three found their audiences.

She was told her leadership style was 'too empathetic' for a fast-moving startup. She disagrees. She thinks empathy is the only leadership style that scales.

Now she advises founders who are tired of being told to toughen up.

name: Rafael Santos
quote: "Your ideas are too far ahead — no one will understand them."
bio:
Rafael has spent 15 years being called a visionary by people who didn't fund him, and a dreamer by people who didn't hire him. He's fine with both.

He works at the edges of technology and culture — not because he likes being contrarian, but because that's where the interesting problems are.

He's currently building something that, by all accounts, shouldn't work. He expects it to.

name: Priya Nair
quote: "You ask too many questions. Just execute."
bio:
Priya has been told she asks too many questions in every room she's ever been in. She considers this her greatest professional achievement.

She's led design and strategy teams across four continents, and her most consistent feedback — across all of them — is that she 'challenges assumptions too aggressively'. She's kept every performance review that says this. She frames it differently: she's the person who asks the thing everyone else is thinking.

She currently runs her own consultancy helping organisations figure out what they actually believe.
